result:
The Cabin on the Crescent Ridge  
—an autumn notebook in sixteen pulses—

1.  
Late September settles on the ridge like a well-loved quilt. The motor sputters its final cough, and the sudden silence is enormous—an in-breath held in the lungs of the world. I step out onto the gravel turnaround and feel gravity change its mind; it now wants to draw everything down the slope toward the lake rather than down through my own feet. A lone, low cloud hooks itself on the fire tower, tugging westward until it is shredded into lace. I have come up here without an agenda beyond no agenda, which is already harder than spreadsheets. That is what I told my colleagues: I’m taking a month off to do nothing. People nodded as if they understood, yet nobody asked how to calendar-stamp nothing.

2.  
Last April at the farmers’ market I found a man selling obsolete maps—gas-station linen from the fifties—creases so deep the rivers looked crucified. I traced the raw umber of State Highway 47, watched its line fray into a dotted wandering near this unnamed ridge. I bought it because-map and all—for three dollars and a promise I’d never laminate it. Some promises are easier kept than others; I have folded it into my breast pocket every week since. Yesterday I unfolded it on the porch table and saw the entire paper now bears the ghost of the fold, as though creasing were a form of writing tracing destinies more permanent than the ink. Between two pleats, a penciled house appeared, though it had not been on the original print. It sat exactly where this cabin stands.

3.  
The structure is an eccentric cousin to those 1970s A-frames: cedar and fieldstone married by carpenters who fell asleep midway through geometry. One wall heads north; another declines south. The roof ridge refuses to meet the ridge of the land so the building lives in mild, cockeyed apology. Yet inside, the beam ribs curve like the hull of a ship and the smell of pitch recalls some long-gone skipper smoking evening pipes. By encircling the loft ladder one draws spirals upward like a nautilus toward a single round window. I leave that window open because I like the idea that sky can wander in without invitation.

4.  
Day two, morning: I drag the foam mattress onto the floor beneath the round window and wake staring through circle into sphere, framed dawn bruising rose into gold. Each model of “I” fits inside the previous one like Russian dolls and something about theapacity through a porthole makes the outermost doll feel laughably unnecessary. Meanwhile, below—literal below—the valley floor is socked in fog thick enough to cough like wool. The treetops emerge as coin-sized islands. Somewhere down there my inbox wins battles against unread counters, but here I have appointed myself treasurer of fog and coin islands, and therefore the metric system changes. Continent becomes cul-de-sac.

5.  
Hunger saunters in wearing his bland bureaucrat suit—nothing dramatic, just a memo: You forgot food. My backpack contains four cans of beans, three oranges, one jar of peanut butter, tea, and a triumphant brick of dark chocolate. I arrange them formally like chess pieces on the table before resigning. The real moves begin: once per day I’ll hike the mile-long gully to the seasonal mailbox where the roadside honor-system farmstand sells eggs and kale and loaves you pay by slipping bills into a mason jar that advertises “In God We Trust.” the phrase appears to be a legal disclaimer. Capital letters like rusted hinges. Yesterday I added an extra five because my bread had a swirl still warm from someone else’s life. God, apparently, is okay with compound interest.

6.  
Afternoons the creek drafts arguments against straight lines. Everything water touches becomes ellipsoid, scalloped, mercurial. I sit on a limestone shelf until my jeans darken with seepage, watching leaf-boats breach miniature rapids. They spin eddies that look, from above, like fingerprints. Each whorl is a signature Dogen might rejoice in. The creek is so modest it has never earned a proper name on any of my obsolete maps. Foundlings often become companions. I assign it “Coracle” because sound of that word feels pocket-sized faith. Tomorrow I will float an actual acorn across and if it successfully navigates the five-meter flume without capsizing, I will interpret that as oracular. My sense of proportion is suddenly pre-socratic.

7.  
Night three, a fox yelps thrice somewhere between first and third base of whatever forest game is underway. The cabin glass vibrates with syllable. The woodstove exhales. From the loft I witness the forest edge blink with fireflies, distinct as syntax. Between pulses dark swells to swallow the next word. From inside my ribcage some other micro-relay captures and re-echoes. I discover I have been broadcasting light my whole life at a frequency my eyes never registered. Perhaps that is true for everyone. Perhaps the fox’s shriek was invitation, and the fireflies are postal workers delivering envelopes of unregistered lumens long thought lost. I unfasten my ribs to sign for them.

8.  
Breakfast mistake. I decided peanut butter plus oatmeal equals higher truth, but forgot about the five-year-old jar whose surface glitters with a vague petroleum sheen. Nostalgia smells safe until acetone speaks. Still, the first spoon is a nutrient ante into memory: junior year apartment, friend Chris with his mismatched chair cult, everybody licking spoons while dancing badly to Fugazi. Such thumbnail resurrection is more caloric than remaining oats. I eat two servings, feel sick, defy sickness by doing push-ups on the 180-year-old floorboards that squeak out scat syllables of protest. They keep tempo better than any fitness app. Around push-up twenty they switch to 7/8 and laughter overtakes me. My chest collapses into giggle heap.

9.  
Thunderstorm midweek––a coliseum of colliding airs. Morning started buttered and sunnyside but then bruise-colored mountains rehearsed curtain call. First thunder is shameless cough of Zeus phlegm huge as Volkswagens. I run outside like some chthonic wedding crasher to strip laundry from the line. Sheets twist into obstinate sails; they want the honeymoon. Rain begins as gravel thrown against tin leaf before dissolving into ropes, curtains, scribbles. I stand shirtless shouting thank you, thank you like receiving ancestor back-pay. When it passes, the world hisses rust and creek is now ten cocky inches deeper. On the trail back up, every mushroom hood shines like a church grotto to capillary miracles.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
